stem: A 28-year-old software engineer presents to your general practice clinic asking about adult ADHD assessment after his partner suggested it. He describes lifelong difficulty completing projects at work, frequently losing his keys, interrupting in meetings, and being unable to sit through long presentations since primary school. School reports from age 9 documented inattention and disruptive behaviour. He has used cannabis socially on weekends for years and drinks four to six standard drinks two nights per week, which he describes as helping his racing thoughts settle. There is no personal history of mood disorder or psychosis. His mother had similar inattention as a child.

data_table.Examination: Mildly fidgety, well groomed, normal mental state examination
data_table.Investigations: Adult Self-Report Scale (ASRS-v1.1) part A: 6 of 6 positive screening items

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in his ADHD assessment?

options.text: Refer to a psychiatrist after a four-week substance-free observation period from alcohol and cannabis
options.rationale: Adult ADHD diagnosis in Australia requires specialist (psychiatrist or paediatrician) confirmation under PBS authority, and DSM-5 requires symptoms are not better explained by substance effects, so a substance-free observation period improves diagnostic accuracy before specialist review. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Practice Guideline 2022)

options.text: Commence methylphenidate immediate release as a four-week diagnostic and therapeutic primary-care trial
options.rationale: Initiating stimulants without specialist diagnosis breaches PBS authority requirements for adult ADHD and exposes a patient with active alcohol and cannabis use to diversion and cardiovascular risk without baseline ECG or substance stabilisation. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Practice Guideline 2022)

options.text: Order brain MRI and quantitative EEG to confirm the diagnosis before any specialist referral
options.rationale: Neuroimaging and quantitative EEG have no validated role in routine adult ADHD diagnosis under the RANZCP guideline; diagnosis remains clinical using DSM-5 criteria with corroborative collateral history. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Practice Guideline 2022)

options.text: Reassure that adult ADHD is unlikely given his tertiary attainment and review routinely in twelve months
options.rationale: Tertiary attainment does not exclude adult ADHD; many adults compensate at high cognitive cost and present in their twenties when role demands exceed compensatory capacity, so this represents a missed diagnosis pathway. (Source: NICE NG87 ADHD: diagnosis and management 2019)
source_refs: NICE NG87 ADHD: diagnosis and management 2019

options.text: Refer directly for cognitive behavioural therapy targeting inattention without prior diagnostic clarification
options.rationale: CBT for ADHD is an evidence-based adjunct but is delivered after diagnostic clarification because the formulation and skills target ADHD-specific deficits; bypassing diagnosis also forfeits stimulant access if criteria are met. (Source: NICE NG87 ADHD: diagnosis and management 2019)
source_refs: NICE NG87 ADHD: diagnosis and management 2019

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD assessment in Australia requires specialist confirmation under PBS authority before stimulant initiation, with DSM-5 explicitly requiring exclusion of substance-attributable symptoms. Concurrent alcohol and cannabis use materially impairs diagnostic accuracy because both can produce inattention and emotional dysregulation that mimic ADHD. Best practice is to refer to a psychiatrist while requesting the patient observe a defined substance-free period to optimise the assessment. Stimulant trials in primary care, neuroimaging, and reflexive reassurance based on educational attainment are all common pitfalls.
key_points: Adult ADHD diagnosis in AU requires psychiatrist or paediatrician confirmation for PBS-authority stimulants
DSM-5 requires symptoms are not better explained by substance use
Cannabis and alcohol both mimic inattention; a four-week substance-free window aids assessment
Neuroimaging and qEEG are not validated diagnostic tools for adult ADHD
explanation.pearls: When a patient drinks four or more standard drinks more than twice weekly or uses cannabis regularly, arrange specialist referral with a planned four-week minimum substance-free assessment window rather than initiating methylphenidate or lisdexamfetamine in primary care.

sources.label: NICE NG87 ADHD: diagnosis and management 2019

stem: A 54-year-old taxi driver presents to your general practice clinic motivated to quit smoking. He has smoked 25 cigarettes per day for 35 years and his last serious quit attempt with patches alone failed at four weeks. He has stable major depressive disorder on sertraline 100 mg daily with no recent suicidal ideation. He has well-controlled hypertension and a history of recurrent vivid dreams when he tried mirtazapine briefly two years ago. He drinks alcohol occasionally, has no renal impairment, and asks for the most effective pharmacotherapy to combine with brief counselling.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 76, BP 132/80, RR 14, SpO2 96% on room air
data_table.Examination: Mood euthymic, no current passive or active suicidal ideation; PHQ-9 score 5
data_table.Investigations: eGFR 88 mL/min/1.73m2; LFTs within reference range
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 100 mg daily; perindopril 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy to recommend?

options.text: Nicotine patch 21 mg daily as monotherapy starting on his planned quit day without short-acting NRT
options.rationale: Single-form NRT has lower quit rates than combination NRT or varenicline and has already failed in this patient; monotherapy patch is not the best next-line choice. (Source: RACGP Supporting smoking cessation 2021)

options.text: Varenicline with standard titration to 1 mg twice daily, commenced one to two weeks before a planned quit day
options.rationale: Varenicline produces the highest single-agent quit rates and is appropriate in stable depression on SSRI; the standard titration to 1 mg twice daily and start one to two weeks pre-quit date allows nicotinic receptor saturation by the quit day. (Source: RACGP Supporting smoking cessation 2021)

options.text: Bupropion titrating from 150 mg daily to 150 mg twice daily, commenced one week before a planned quit day
options.rationale: Bupropion is a reasonable option but its combination with sertraline raises serotonergic and seizure-threshold considerations, and head-to-head data favour varenicline for quit-rate efficacy. (Source: RACGP Supporting smoking cessation 2021)

options.text: Nortriptyline 75 mg nocte for cessation after cross-tapering sertraline off over the preceding fortnight
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is a second-line cessation option with worse tolerability and would require disrupting effective antidepressant therapy without sound clinical justification. (Source: RACGP Supporting smoking cessation 2021)

options.text: Defer pharmacotherapy and refer for hypnotherapy and acupuncture as first-line cessation interventions
options.rationale: Hypnotherapy and acupuncture lack robust evidence for sustained cessation and deferring proven pharmacotherapy in a motivated heavy smoker reduces the probability of quitting. (Source: RACGP Supporting smoking cessation 2021)

explanation.summary: Varenicline remains the most efficacious single-agent smoking cessation pharmacotherapy in Australian primary care and is appropriate in patients with stable, well-treated depression. Standard titration is 0.5 mg once daily for three days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for four days, then 1 mg twice daily, commenced one to two weeks before the planned quit day. Combination NRT (patch plus short-acting) is the alternative when varenicline is contraindicated or not tolerated. Bupropion and nortriptyline are second-line and complicated by interactions with SSRIs.
key_points: Varenicline > combination NRT > single NRT > bupropion > nortriptyline for quit-rate efficacy
Standard titration ends at 1 mg twice daily; start one to two weeks before quit day
Stable, well-managed depression is not a contraindication to varenicline under RACGP guidance
Patient has already failed monotherapy patch; do not repeat the same approach
explanation.pearls: For a motivated heavy smoker with previous patch-only failure, prescribe varenicline started one to two weeks before quit day rather than repeating single-form NRT - PBS subsidises 12 weeks of varenicline once per year with twice-yearly access criteria documented in the RACGP guideline.

stem: A 27-year-old man with a first episode of schizophrenia commenced olanzapine 10 mg nightly six weeks ago in your community mental health clinic. He attends for routine review feeling more settled with reduced auditory hallucinations, but reports increased appetite and a 4 kg weight gain. He has no personal or family history of diabetes. He has not had any pre-treatment baseline metabolic bloods taken because he was acutely unwell at initiation. He smokes ten cigarettes per day and drinks no alcohol. He asks how often you will be checking his physical health.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84, BP 128/78, RR 14, SpO2 99%, weight 86 kg (baseline 82 kg), waist 96 cm
data_table.Examination: Morning sedation only, no abnormal movements, BMI 27.8
data_table.Investigations: Pending: fasting lipids, fasting glucose or HbA1c, prolactin

lead_in: Which metabolic monitoring schedule best matches Australian best-practice for olanzapine?

options.text: Fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipids and waist at three months and then twelve-monthly while stable
options.rationale: Three-month intervals miss the peak window for olanzapine-induced metabolic change, which occurs in the first 12 weeks; the recommended early review is at 6 and 12 weeks. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016

options.text: Fasting lipids only at six months with weight and waist circumference checked at twelve-monthly intervals
options.rationale: This schedule under-monitors glucose despite olanzapine being among the highest-risk antipsychotics for new-onset diabetes; weight at annual intervals also misses early intervention windows. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016

options.text: Weight and waist at every visit; fasting glucose or HbA1c and fasting lipids at baseline, twelve weeks, then yearly
options.rationale: Baseline (because none was done), 12-week reassessment, and at least annual review thereafter is the RANZCP-endorsed minimum schedule for high metabolic-risk antipsychotics like olanzapine, with weight and waist at each contact. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016

options.text: ECG and prolactin only at six and twelve months without routine glucose or lipid testing during the first year
options.rationale: Olanzapine has minimal QT impact and low prolactin elevation; the priority metabolic targets (glucose, lipids, weight) are not addressed by this schedule. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016

options.text: Two-yearly OGTT only, with no routine fasting lipid, weight or waist monitoring required during the interval
options.rationale: Two-yearly OGTT alone misses early diabetes and dyslipidaemia and abandons weight and waist monitoring, which are the most sensitive early warning signs of metabolic harm. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016

explanation.summary: Olanzapine is among the highest metabolic-risk antipsychotics; the RANZCP schizophrenia clinical practice guideline recommends a structured monitoring schedule with weight and waist at every contact and biochemical screening at baseline, 12 weeks, and then yearly while stable. Early intervention windows are clustered in the first 12 weeks when most weight gain and dysglycaemia emerge. Because this patient had no baseline bloods at initiation, repeating them at 6 weeks and again at 12 weeks (then annually) is the pragmatic catch-up plan. Failure to monitor is one of the most common quality gaps in community psychosis care.
key_points: Olanzapine and clozapine are highest metabolic-risk antipsychotics
Weight and waist at every visit; fasting glucose/HbA1c and lipids at baseline, 12 weeks, then annually
Most metabolic change happens in the first 12 weeks
Switching to a lower-risk agent (aripiprazole, lurasidone) is reasonable if metabolic harm emerges
explanation.pearls: If weight gain exceeds 5 percent of baseline in the first 12 weeks of olanzapine, this triggers a switch discussion or addition of metformin per RANZCP guidance, not a wait-and-see at the annual review.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016

stem: A 52-year-old accountant is referred to your community mental health clinic by her GP. For the past 18 months she has held a fixed, unshakeable belief that her next-door neighbour is poisoning her water supply through the shared plumbing because she once parked across his driveway. She has had her water independently tested three times with normal results, but remains convinced. Outside this single belief she works full time, manages her finances, maintains friendships, and presents at interview with normal speech, organised thought, and full affective range. She denies hallucinations, has no negative symptoms, and there has never been a manic or depressive episode. She does not use substances and her medical history is unremarkable.

data_table.Examination: Mental state examination otherwise unremarkable apart from the persecutory belief; cognition intact; insight absent for the belief only
data_table.Investigations: TSH, B12, folate, electrolytes within reference range; urine drug screen negative

lead_in: Which DSM-5 diagnosis best fits the presentation?

options.text: Paranoid schizophrenia given the fixed persecutory belief and the disturbed neighbour interaction over months
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires at least one of delusions, hallucinations, or disorganised speech plus a second criterion-A symptom, with functional decline lasting at least six months; she has none of these except an isolated delusion and no functional decline. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder, persecutory subtype, given the discrete structure of the delusional belief
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder requires symptoms lasting more than a day and less than one month with eventual full return to premorbid function; her belief has persisted 18 months which exceeds this threshold. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder, given the total symptom duration shorter than two years at presentation
options.rationale: Schizophreniform disorder requires criterion-A psychotic symptoms (e.g. hallucinations, disorganised speech) lasting between one and six months, neither of which fits this duration or symptom profile. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

options.text: Delusional disorder, persecutory type, given a single non-bizarre delusion for at least one month with preserved function
options.rationale: DSM-5 delusional disorder requires one or more delusions for at least one month, criterion A for schizophrenia not otherwise met, and apart from the delusion impact, function is not markedly impaired and behaviour is not obviously bizarre - this matches her presentation. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

options.text: Obsessive-compulsive disorder with poor insight, given her sustained preoccupation with the neighbour situation
options.rationale: OCD with poor insight involves obsessions experienced as intrusive and unwanted plus compulsive rituals, neither of which she describes; her belief is held with full conviction as reality, not as an unwanted intrusive thought. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

explanation.summary: Delusional disorder is characterised by one or more delusions lasting at least one month, no other criterion-A psychotic symptoms, preserved function outside the delusion and absence of bizarre behaviour. The persecutory subtype is the most common. It is differentiated from schizophrenia by the absence of hallucinations, disorganisation and functional decline, from schizophreniform by duration and symptom profile, and from OCD with poor insight by the ego-syntonic belief held as fact rather than an intrusive obsession. First-line treatment is an antipsychotic at modest doses with attention to therapeutic alliance.
key_points: DSM-5 delusional disorder: one or more delusions for at least one month
Function preserved outside the delusion; behaviour not obviously bizarre
Schizophrenia requires two criterion-A symptoms with six-month duration
OCD with poor insight: obsessions are intrusive and ego-dystonic
explanation.pearls: When function is preserved and a single non-bizarre belief is held with absolute conviction for over a month, think delusional disorder before schizophrenia; the treatment course often starts with a low dose atypical antipsychotic (e.g. risperidone 1-2 mg) with longitudinal review.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia 2016

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 68-year-old woman admitted for severe major depressive disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features. She has lost 9 kg over six weeks, refuses food intermittently believing it is contaminated, and has stopped drinking adequately for the past 48 hours. She has not responded to sertraline 200 mg daily for six weeks. Past medical history includes essential hypertension on perindopril, mild aortic stenosis (peak gradient 28 mmHg, EF 60%), and degenerative cervical spine disease. She was treated with right unilateral ECT 12 years ago for a similar episode with full recovery and no significant side effects. Family and patient consent for further treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88 sinus, BP 122/76, RR 16, SpO2 97%, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Profoundly slowed, food refusal, mute episodes, dry mucosae
data_table.Investigations: Na 132, K 4.0, urea 9.2, creatinine 86, eGFR 64, albumin 30, ECG sinus QTc 430 ms; recent transthoracic echocardiogram unchanged
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 200 mg daily, perindopril 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next treatment step?

options.text: Switch sertraline to an SNRI and review response over a further four weeks before considering escalation steps
options.rationale: Sequential antidepressant trials over four weeks are inappropriate given imminent nutritional and hydration risk, food-related delusions, and prior ECT response; this delays definitive treatment. (Source: RANZCP Professional Practice Guideline 13 ECT 2019)

options.text: Add olanzapine 5 mg nocte for psychotic features and reassess clinical response over four weeks of combination treatment
options.rationale: While antipsychotic augmentation is appropriate for psychotic depression, monotherapy or augmentation alone over four weeks risks dehydration and death; ECT is faster and supported by prior response. (Source: RANZCP Professional Practice Guideline 13 ECT 2019)

options.text: Commence intravenous ketamine 0.5 mg/kg over 40 minutes twice weekly as the principal antidepressant therapy
options.rationale: Ketamine is not standard initial therapy for psychotic depression and is contraindicated in active psychotic features in most Australian protocols; it is not an evidence-based alternative to ECT in this setting. (Source: RANZCP Professional Practice Guideline 13 ECT 2019)

options.text: Proceed to bitemporal high-dose ECT first-line after anaesthetic review, given the severity and aim for fastest response
options.rationale: ECT is the right modality but in this patient with documented response to prior right unilateral treatment, repeating right unilateral first is preferred unless a faster initial response is required; bitemporal first-line is not mandated. (Source: RANZCP Professional Practice Guideline 13 ECT 2019)

options.text: Proceed to right unilateral high-dose ECT after anaesthetic review and informed consent with cardiac and spine precautions
options.rationale: ECT is the treatment of choice for severe psychotic depression with food refusal and nutritional risk, particularly in an older adult with prior ECT response. Right unilateral high-dose is appropriate first electrode placement given previous tolerance; anaesthetic review addresses her mild aortic stenosis and cervical spine. (Source: RANZCP Professional Practice Guideline 13 ECT 2019)

explanation.summary: ECT is the most effective treatment for severe psychotic depression in older adults and is indicated when food and fluid refusal create imminent medical risk. Right unilateral high-dose placement is the recommended first electrode position in patients with prior unilateral response because it preserves cognitive outcome while delivering high efficacy. Anaesthetic considerations such as mild aortic stenosis and cervical spine disease require formal pre-anaesthetic review and positioning planning but are not contraindications. Informed consent, capacity assessment and family involvement remain core process steps.
key_points: ECT first-line for severe psychotic depression with food refusal or imminent risk
Prior unilateral response favours right unilateral high-dose first
Aortic stenosis and cervical spine disease require anaesthetic planning, not exclusion
Capacity, consent and substitute-decision pathways apply
explanation.pearls: When a depressed older adult with psychotic features stops eating and drinking for 48 hours, escalate to ECT planning the same day rather than another sequential antidepressant trial; expect first response by sessions three to five and remission by session eight to twelve.

stem: You are the ED registrar called to see a 32-year-old woman brought in 90 minutes after ingesting around 2 g of amitriptyline. She was alert on arrival but is now drowsy with GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5). She vomited once en route. There is no co-ingestion reported. The triage ECG taken 15 minutes ago is repeated now and shows sinus tachycardia at 122 with QRS 118 ms (up from 96 ms), R wave in aVR 4 mm, and terminal R/S ratio in aVR greater than 0.7. BP is 96/58 and dropping. Activated charcoal was not given.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 122, BP 96/58, RR 22, SpO2 95% room air, Temp 37.1
data_table.Examination: GCS 11, dry mucosae, pupils 5 mm sluggish, no focal neurology, decreased bowel sounds
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.30, HCO3 18, lactate 3.2; Na 138; K 3.6; paracetamol level pending; ECG as in stem

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, repeat to target arterial pH 7.50-7.55 and QRS narrowing
options.rationale: QRS prolongation greater than 100 ms with progression, hypotension and R in aVR greater than 3 mm are indications for sodium bicarbonate boluses to alkalinise blood and provide sodium loading, reducing cardiotoxicity. Target pH 7.50-7.55 with monitoring. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology 2020)

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg titrated to reversal of sedation while monitoring continuously for seizure activity
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in TCA overdose because it precipitates seizures by unmasking benzodiazepine-suppressed proconvulsant activity and offers no benefit on cardiotoxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology 2020)

options.text: Administer intravenous procainamide 100 mg over five minutes to treat the conduction delay and protect against arrhythmia
options.rationale: Class IA antiarrhythmics worsen sodium channel blockade and risk asystole in TCA poisoning; they are absolutely contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology 2020)

options.text: Administer intravenous physostigmine 1 mg to reverse anticholinergic features and improve haemodynamic status promptly
options.rationale: Physostigmine has precipitated asystole and seizures in TCA overdose and is not used for the anticholinergic toxidrome in this setting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology 2020)

options.text: Give activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube without first securing the airway despite the declining conscious level
options.rationale: Charcoal is relatively unhelpful at 90 minutes post-ingestion and is hazardous in a patient with declining GCS without a secured airway because of aspiration risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology 2020)

explanation.summary: TCA overdose cardiotoxicity is sodium-channel blockade producing QRS prolongation, hypotension and tall R in aVR. Sodium bicarbonate boluses (1-2 mmol/kg titrated to QRS narrowing and arterial pH 7.50-7.55) are the cornerstone of management together with airway protection, benzodiazepines for seizures, and fluids or noradrenaline for hypotension. Class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics, flumazenil, and physostigmine are contraindicated. Charcoal is selectively useful very early and only with airway control.
key_points: QRS greater than 100 ms or R in aVR greater than 3 mm: give sodium bicarbonate
Target arterial pH 7.50-7.55 with serial ECG and ABG monitoring
Avoid flumazenil, physostigmine and class IA antiarrhythmics
Hypotension responds to fluids then noradrenaline; lipid emulsion is rescue
explanation.pearls: Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg as a bolus narrows QRS within minutes; if QRS does not narrow despite adequate alkalinisation and sodium loading, escalate to intralipid 20 percent 1.5 mL/kg in refractory TCA cardiotoxicity per Toxicology and Toxinology guideline.

stem: A 24-year-old woman with borderline personality disorder is being followed in your community psychiatry team. She has been in dialectical behaviour therapy for nine months. Over the past two weeks she has presented to ED four times after superficial self-harm episodes, each precipitated by escalating arguments with her partner. She has skipped two of her last four group skills sessions because she found them dysregulating. There has been no recent suicide attempt or hospitalisation, no substance use, and her mood has been reactive but no major depressive episode. She is on no psychotropics.

data_table.Examination: Healed superficial linear cuts to bilateral forearms; mental state - dysregulated affect, no current suicidal intent, full reality testing

lead_in: Within standard DBT, which target should the next individual therapy session prioritise?

options.text: Sleep hygiene and exercise scheduling as foundational stage-one quality-of-life targets within DBT this week
options.rationale: Quality-of-life targets sit below life-threatening behaviours and therapy-interfering behaviours in DBT's stage-one hierarchy; they are not the next priority here. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012

options.text: Address life-threatening behaviours by completing a behavioural chain analysis of the most recent ED self-harm episode
options.rationale: Life-threatening behaviours are top of the hierarchy but the question asks which target to prioritise next given multiple targets are active; a single chain analysis without sequencing the next steps does not fully match DBT structure. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012

options.text: Address therapy-interfering behaviours first by problem-solving her skipped skills group sessions before any other target
options.rationale: Therapy-interfering behaviours are addressed only after life-threatening behaviours in the Linehan stage-one hierarchy; reordering risks reinforcing avoidance of the most dangerous target. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012

options.text: Address life-threatening behaviours first, then therapy-interfering behaviour (missed group), then quality-of-life targets
options.rationale: Standard Linehan DBT stage-one hierarchy is life-threatening behaviours, then therapy-interfering behaviours, then quality-of-life-interfering behaviours, then skills acquisition; this sequence allows the highest-risk target to be addressed first while still attending to group attendance. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily as the priority intervention for repeated affective dysregulation episodes leading to self-harm
options.rationale: Australian and NHMRC guidance does not recommend antidepressants as first-line for BPD-related dysregulation in the absence of a comorbid mood or anxiety disorder; pharmacotherapy is adjunctive at best. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012

explanation.summary: Standard Linehan DBT stage-one targets are ordered as life-threatening behaviours, then therapy-interfering behaviours, then quality-of-life-interfering behaviours, then skills acquisition. A patient with escalating self-harm who is also missing skills group needs chain analysis of the self-harm first, followed by problem-solving the group avoidance, before broader QoL work. Pharmacotherapy in BPD is adjunctive; NHMRC and RANZCP guidance discourage antidepressant initiation for affective instability in the absence of comorbid depression or anxiety.
key_points: DBT stage-one hierarchy: life-threat -> therapy-interfering -> QoL -> skills
Chain analysis is the core individual-therapy technique for self-harm
Antidepressants are not first-line BPD pharmacotherapy per NHMRC
Missed group is a therapy-interfering behaviour addressed after life-threat
explanation.pearls: When a BPD patient self-harms repeatedly and also skips DBT group, run the chain analysis on the self-harm first within the individual session and only then problem-solve the group attendance gap; reordering the hierarchy reinforces avoidance.

sources.label: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD 2012
RANZCP Practice Note: Psychotropic medications and BPD 2018

subtopic_detail: Anorexia nervosa - medical risk and refeeding
stem: A 17-year-old female presents to your hospital paediatric medical ward via ED at her parents' request after eight months of restrictive eating. Weight is 38 kg (BMI 13.6), with rapid weight loss of 6 kg in the past month. She is bradycardic at 38 bpm, normotensive supine but BP drops from 100/64 to 78/52 on standing with a 32 bpm rise. She is cold, mildly slowed cognitively, and her ECG shows sinus bradycardia with QTc 470 ms. Bloods show K 3.0, phosphate 0.62 mmol/L, magnesium 0.68 mmol/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L. The dietitian proposes initial caloric intake of 1400 kcal per day with stepwise increases.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 38 lying, BP 100/64 lying to 78/52 standing; postural HR rise 32; Temp 35.4; SpO2 97%
data_table.Examination: Significant muscle wasting, lanugo, peripheral oedema absent, mild bradypsychism
data_table.Investigations: K 3.0, phosphate 0.62, magnesium 0.68, glucose 3.2, urea 6.0, creatinine 70, eGFR 92, albumin 35, LFTs ALT 64, INR 1.1; ECG sinus bradycardia QTc 470 ms; ketones positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate refeeding plan in the first 48 hours?

options.text: Commence oral feeding at 2200 kcal per day from day one with no prophylactic electrolyte replacement and ward observation
options.rationale: Caloric loading of this magnitude without electrolyte correction risks fatal refeeding syndrome with phosphate crashes and arrhythmia in a high-risk patient. (Source: InsideOut MARSIPAN Australia 2020)

options.text: Feed at 1400 kcal/day on cardiac-monitored bed with prophylactic phosphate, potassium, magnesium plus IV thiamine before feeds
options.rationale: Australian MARSIPAN and RANZCP guidance recommend starting at moderate caloric intake (around 1400 kcal/day in severe cases), prophylactic phosphate/K/Mg replacement, thiamine before feeding, monitored bed and daily bloods to mitigate refeeding syndrome. (Source: InsideOut MARSIPAN Australia 2020)

options.text: Initiate parenteral nutrition via a central venous line as primary route given poor oral intake and severe malnutrition
options.rationale: PN is rarely required and adds infection and refeeding risk; the gut should be used wherever possible with NG support if oral intake is inadequate. (Source: InsideOut MARSIPAN Australia 2020)

options.text: Delay any feeding for 48 hours to allow serum electrolytes to fully normalise before nutrition is reintroduced safely
options.rationale: Prolonged starvation worsens cardiac, hepatic, and immune outcomes and increases mortality risk; feeding should be initiated while replacing electrolytes concurrently. (Source: InsideOut MARSIPAN Australia 2020)

options.text: Discharge home with weekly outpatient dietitian and GP review given she is awake, cooperative and willing to engage
options.rationale: Multiple severity markers (BMI 13.6, HR 38, postural drop with HR rise 32, QTc 470 ms, hypokalaemia and hypophosphataemia) mandate inpatient medical stabilisation. (Source: InsideOut MARSIPAN Australia 2020)

explanation.summary: Severe anorexia nervosa with multiple cardiometabolic markers (BMI under 14, bradycardia under 40, marked postural change, QTc prolongation, hypokalaemia and hypophosphataemia) requires inpatient medical stabilisation. Australian MARSIPAN and RANZCP guidance recommend cautious caloric initiation around 1400 kcal/day for the most severe cases with prophylactic phosphate, potassium and magnesium replacement, thiamine before any feeding, cardiac monitoring and daily electrolyte review. PN is avoided where possible. The greatest mortality is from refeeding syndrome in the first week and from cardiac arrhythmia.
key_points: MARSIPAN severity markers include BMI <14, HR <40, postural BP fall with HR rise >20, QTc >450 ms
Start refeeding around 1400 kcal/day in highest-risk cases with stepwise increase
Thiamine 100 mg IV daily for 3-5 days before and during refeeding
Replace phosphate, K and Mg prophylactically; check daily for first week
explanation.pearls: If serum phosphate falls below 0.5 mmol/L within 72 hours of refeeding, replace IV phosphate (typically 0.3-0.6 mmol/kg over 6-12 hours) and pause caloric increases until phosphate is stable per MARSIPAN Australia.

sources.label: RANZCP Eating Disorders Clinical Practice Guideline 2014

stem: A 38-year-old woman with newly diagnosed rheumatoid arthritis is planned to commence adalimumab through your rheumatology clinic. She was born in Vietnam, arrived in Australia six years ago, and received BCG vaccination as a child. She has had no known TB contacts and no respiratory symptoms. She has no chronic liver disease and her bloods are normal. The rheumatologist asks you to organise pre-biologic latent tuberculosis screening.

data_table.Examination: BCG scar present; chest clear; no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: FBC, LFTs, renal function within reference range; CXR pending
data_table.Medications: Methotrexate 15 mg weekly, folate 5 mg weekly

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate screening test for latent TB before starting a TNF-alpha inhibitor?

options.text: Tuberculin skin test (Mantoux) alone using a 5 mm threshold for positivity in this immunosuppressed cohort
options.rationale: TST is influenced by prior BCG vaccination; in a BCG-vaccinated patient, an interferon-gamma release assay is preferred because it is unaffected by BCG. (Source: NTAC IGRA Position Statement 2018)

options.text: QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus interferon-gamma release assay together with a baseline plain chest radiograph
options.rationale: IGRA (e.g. QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus) is the preferred screen in BCG-vaccinated individuals before TNF-alpha inhibitors and is paired with CXR to evaluate for active disease before treatment of latent infection. (Source: NTAC IGRA Position Statement 2018)

options.text: Three serial induced-sputum smears for acid-fast bacilli over consecutive mornings as the principal screen
options.rationale: Sputum AFB is for suspected active TB with respiratory symptoms; it is not the screening test for latent TB in an asymptomatic patient. (Source: NTAC IGRA Position Statement 2018)

options.text: High-resolution CT of the chest as the primary screen for asymptomatic latent tuberculosis before biologic therapy
options.rationale: HRCT does not identify latent infection; latent TB is by definition asymptomatic and radiographically silent and requires immunological detection. (Source: NTAC IGRA Position Statement 2018)

options.text: Total serum IgE and peripheral blood absolute eosinophil count to risk-stratify for mycobacterial disease activity
options.rationale: IgE and eosinophils have no role in TB screening; they relate to allergic and parasitic disease. (Source: NTAC IGRA Position Statement 2018)

explanation.summary: Pre-biologic TB screening in Australia uses an interferon-gamma release assay (typically QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus) in BCG-vaccinated patients because TST has poor specificity after BCG. CXR is performed concurrently to exclude active disease before commencing latent TB therapy. If positive, isoniazid for nine months (or rifampicin 4 months or 3HP regimen via specialist clinic) is offered with monthly LFT monitoring. Untreated latent TB carries a markedly elevated reactivation risk on TNF-alpha inhibitors.
key_points: IGRA preferred over TST in BCG-vaccinated patients per NTAC
Always pair IGRA with CXR before treating latent TB
TNF-alpha inhibitors increase reactivation risk substantially
Standard latent TB therapy: isoniazid 9 months or rifampicin 4 months
explanation.pearls: Use QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus, not Mantoux, in any patient born in a high-incidence country who received BCG; if positive and CXR clear, give isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months with pyridoxine 25 mg daily and monthly LFTs.

stem: A 72-year-old retired teacher is referred to your geriatric clinic with a six-month history of asymmetric rest tremor of the right hand, micrographia, slowed gait with reduced right arm swing, and difficulty turning in bed. He has fallen once. He has well-controlled hypertension and stage 3 chronic kidney disease. Examination confirms cogwheel rigidity right greater than left, bradykinesia and a positive pull test for postural instability. There is no early autonomic failure and no cognitive complaint. He scores 28/30 on MoCA. MRI brain is unremarkable. He asks about treatment options to maintain independence.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 68, BP 128/76 supine to 124/74 standing; no postural drop
data_table.Examination: Asymmetric rest tremor right hand, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability; UPDRS motor moderate

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Pramipexole modified release titrated to 1.5 mg daily as initial dopamine agonist monotherapy for motor symptoms
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists are associated with higher rates of impulse-control disorders, hallucinations, ankle oedema and sleep attacks in older adults; levodopa is preferred over agonists as first-line in patients over 70. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology 2017)

options.text: Levodopa with carbidopa (Sinemet) 100/25 mg three times daily titrated upward to motor response over weeks
options.rationale: Levodopa combined with a peripheral decarboxylase inhibitor is the most effective symptomatic therapy and is preferred first-line in patients over 70 because of greater motor benefit, fewer neuropsychiatric complications than agonists, and lower fall risk on titration. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology 2017)

options.text: Trihexyphenidyl 2 mg twice daily as initial anticholinergic monotherapy directed primarily at his rest tremor
options.rationale: Anticholinergics in older adults precipitate confusion, urinary retention and cognitive decline; they are not used first-line in patients over 70 with Parkinson's disease. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology 2017)

options.text: Amantadine 100 mg twice daily as oral monotherapy for combined bradykinesia, rigidity and rest tremor relief
options.rationale: Amantadine has modest motor benefit and is mainly used for dyskinesia later in the disease; its renal clearance and CNS side-effect profile make it unattractive first-line in a 72-year-old with CKD. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology 2017)

options.text: Rasagiline 1 mg daily as MAO-B inhibitor monotherapy aiming for modest symptomatic effect in early disease
options.rationale: MAO-B inhibitors have modest symptomatic effect and may be appropriate in early mild disease in younger patients, but in this patient with established motor disability and postural instability, levodopa offers superior symptom control. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology 2017)

explanation.summary: Levodopa combined with carbidopa is the most effective symptomatic therapy for Parkinson's disease and is the preferred first-line agent in older patients (over 70) where dopamine agonists carry higher risks of impulse control disorders, hallucinations and falls. Dose is titrated slowly to motor response; the goal in early disease is the lowest effective dose. Long-term motor complications such as dyskinesia and wearing-off are managed with adjuncts including COMT inhibitors and MAO-B inhibitors. Multidisciplinary care, falls prevention and bone health complete the management plan.
key_points: Levodopa first-line in patients aged over 70
Dopamine agonists more often first-line in younger patients with mild disease
Avoid anticholinergics in older adults
Multidisciplinary input - physiotherapy, OT, speech pathology - improves outcomes
explanation.pearls: In a 72-year-old with postural instability and falls, choose Sinemet 100/25 mg three times daily titrated to motor response rather than pramipexole, since the absolute risk of falls and impulse control disorder on agonist therapy outweighs the modest motor-complication advantage.

sources.label: Movement Disorder Society Evidence-Based Medicine Review Update 2018
